text: Society everywhere is in conspiracy against the manhood of every one of its members. Society is a joint-stock company, in which the members agree, for the better securing of his bread to each shareholder, to surrender the liberty and culture of the eater. The virtue in most request is conformity. Self-reliance is its aversion. It loves not realities and creators, but names and customs.
author: Ralph Waldo Emerson
locator: Essay "Self-Reliance" (Essays: First Series), paragraph beginning "These are the voices which we hear in solitude"
source: Project Gutenberg plain text, eBook #16643 (Essays: First Series)
out_pulls_note: Out-pulls the gloss: the gloss says 'trust the self over the crowd'; this passage gives Emerson's argument for WHY (society as a conformity-demanding joint-stock company) rather than restating the conclusion.
search_notes: Verified against Emerson's essay 'Self-Reliance' in Gutenberg #16643 (Essays: First Series), not Thoreau's Walden — Emerson is the namesake and I byte-verified him. This Gutenberg text inserts bracketed footnote markers (e.g. [156], [165], [167]) inline, which would break a byte match; I chose this footnote-free paragraph rather than the 'Trust thyself[156]' or 'nonconformist[165]' lines. Byte-verified verbatim. Canonical Emerson; no misattribution risk. Avoided verdict words per settled=false.
